CS542200 Parallel Programming  
Homework 4: FlashAttention  
Due: Dec 7 23:59, 2025

1. # **Goal**

This assignment focuses on implementing the forward pass of the FlashAttention. You will gain an understanding of how efficient attention mechanisms work and how CUDA can be utilized to accelerate them. In this assignment, you will realize the performance benefits of optimizing the attention computation on GPUs. Finally, you are encouraged to explore and apply additional optimization strategies to maximize performance .

2. # **Requirements**

* In this assignment, you are asked to implement a CUDA-based program for the forward pass of the FlashAttention.  
  * Your implementation must leverage CUDA to compute the forward pass of FlashAttention.  
  * You need to implement using the core concepts of the FlashAttention algorithm, including kernel fusion and tiling techniques.  
  * You are encouraged to optimize program performance by adjusting the data replacement and parallelism levels.  
  * The program must correctly handle memory management, kernel launches, and ensure the correctness of the attention computation.

3. # **FlashAttention algorithm**

In this assignment, You are asked to implement the FlashAttention algorithm. Below is the algorithm overview:

* Inputs  
  * Q, K, V  RN\*d : Query, Key, and Value matrices in HBM (High Bandwidth Memory).  
* Outputs  
  * O  RN\*d :  Output matrices.

![][image1]

**FlashAttention Algorithm Explanation:**

The FlashAttention algorithm is designed to efficiently compute scaled dot-product attention by leveraging GPU memory hierarchy, which includes High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) and on-chip SRAM. This method minimizes memory read and write operations, addressing the bottlenecks associated with memory-bound attention computations.

In FlashAttention, the input matrices Q , K, and V (queries, keys, and values) are divided into blocks of manageable sizes. This ensures that intermediate computations can be performed within the on-chip SRAM, which has significantly higher bandwidth compared to HBM. The algorithm operates in two main loops:

1. **Outer Loop:** Blocks of K and V are copied from HBM to SRAM.  
2. **Inner Loop:** Each block of Q is loaded into SRAM, and the attention matrix (QK^T) is computed block-wise. This matrix undergoes row-wise max normalization and exponentiation to ensure numerical stability. The normalized attention scores are then used to compute the weighted sum of V, resulting in the output matrix O.

The key optimization lies in performing all the computationally intensive operations (e.g., matrix multiplications, softmax) directly on SRAM to reduce the frequency of accessing HBM. This optimization not only accelerates computation but also reduces energy consumption.

**Paper link:**

[https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.14135](https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.14135)

4. # **Run your programs**

* ## **Preparation (only need to execute it once at the beginning)**

| *\# On Apollo GPU* mkdir \-p \~/hw4 cp /home/pp25/share/hw4/seq-attention.c \~/hw4 cp /home/pp25/share/hw4/seq-flashattention.c \~/hw4 cp /home/pp25/share/hw4/Makefile \~/hw4 |
| :---- |

* ## **Command line specification**

| *\# On Nvidia GPU* srun \-p nvidia \-N1 \-n1 \--gres=gpu:1 ./hw4 INPUTFILE OUTPUTFILE *\# On AMD GPU* srun \-p amd \-N1 \-n1 \--gres=gpu:1 ./hw4 INPUTFILE OUTPUTFILE |
| :---- |

  * INPUTFILE: The pathname of the input file. Your program should read the input matrix from this file.  
    * Nvidia GPU : /home/pp25/share/hw4/testcases  
    * AMD GPU : /nvme/testcases/hw4/  
  * OUTPUTFILE: The pathname of the output file. Your program should output the output matrix to this file.   
* **Input specification**  
  * The input file is a binary file containing 32-bit integers (*B, N, D*) and 32-bit floating-point numbers (*Q, K, V*).  
  * The first three integers are *the batch size (B)*, *the sequence length (N)* and *the embedding size (d)*.  
  * Then, there are *B* batches. Each batch consists of *Query (Q)*, *Key (K)*, and *Value (V)*  matrices:

    1. ## *Query (Q)*: *N \* d* floating-point numbers

    2. ## *Key (K)*: *N \* d* floating-point numbers

    3. ## *Value (V)*: *N \* d* floating-point numbers

  * ## The ranges for the input are:

    * 2B14000  
    * N{128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768}  
    * d{32, 64}  
    * \-3.0Qi, j, Ki, j, Vi, j3.0  
    * B\*N\*d\<56000000  
* **Output specification**  
  * The output file is also in binary format.  
  * You should output an output file containing B\*N\*d floating-point numbers.

5. # **Report**

Answer the questions below. You are recommended to use the same section numbering as they are listed.

1. **Implementation**   
   1. Describe how you implemented the FlashAttention forward pass using CUDA. Mention the algorithm's key steps, such as matrix blocking, SRAM usage, and how intermediate results like scaling factors (ℓ and 𝑚) were calculated.  
   2. Explain how matrices Q, K, and V are divided into blocks and processed in parallel.  
   3. Describe how you chose the block sizes B\_r​ and B\_c​ and why.  
   4. Specify the configurations for CUDA kernel launches, such as the number of threads per block, shared memory allocation, and grid dimensions.  
   5. Justify your choices and how they relate to the blocking factors and the SRAM size.  
2. **Profiling Results**  
   Provide the profiling results of following metrics on the kernel of your program using NVIDIA profiling tools. [NVIDIA Profiler Guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/profiler-users-guide/index.html).  
   * occupancy  
     * sm efficiency  
     * shared memory load/store throughput  
     * global load/store throughput

| Note:To run nvprof on Apollo GPU with flags like \--metrics, please run on the slurm partition nvidia. e.g. srun \-p nvidia \-N1 \-n1 \--gres=gpu:1 nvprof \--metrics gld\_throughput ./hw4 /home/pp25/share/hw4/testcases/t01 t01.out For more details, please refer to the [Nvprof Transition Guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/NsightComputeCli/index.html#nvprof-guide). |
| :---- |

3. **Experiment & Analysis**  
   1. System Spec  
      State which platform you used to conduct your experiment. If you didn’t use our Apollo-GPU server for the experiments, please show the CPU, RAM, disk of the system.  
   2. Optimization  
      Any optimizations after you port the algorithm on GPU, describe them with sentences and charts. Here are some techniques you can implement:  
      * Coalesced memory access  
      * Shared memory  
      * Handle bank conflict  
      * CUDA 2D alignment  
      * Occupancy optimization  
      * Streaming  
   3. Others  
      Additional charts with explanation and studies. The more, the better.  
4. **AMD GPU Porting and Analysis**  
   1. Implementation

   Explain how you ported the CUDA code to HIP code.

   2. Experiment & Analyze

   You must specify which profiling tools were used for each of your experiments.

      * Use profiling tools such as rocprof and rocprof-compute to analyze performance, and identify the differences between GTX 1080 and MI100 when running equivalent code. Discuss the reasons behind these differences (hint: hardware architecture differences?).  
      * For the AMD GPU, perform **one optimization** that was not done on the NVIDIA GPU, or **modify one NVIDIA-specific optimization** to suit the AMD GPU. Explain and show why this optimization is effective or ineffective, and clarify why it is specific to the AMD GPU.  
      * Conduct experiments on the AMD GPU, and compare as well as discuss the results between the NVIDIA and AMD GPUs.  
   3. Others

   Additional charts with explanation and studies. The more, the better.

5. **Experience & conclusion**  
   1. What have you learned from this homework?  
   2. Feedback (optional)

6. # **Grading**

1. **\[60%\] Correctness (NV-GPU version only)**

A total of 30 test cases, from t01 to t30, will be used to test your implementation.

* You get 60 points if you passed all the test cases, max(0,60-3k) points if there are k failed test cases.  
* Each test case has a time limit.  
  * t01 \- t10: 2 seconds.  
  * t11 \- t20: 6 seconds.  
  * t21 \- t30: 10 seconds.  
* The floating-point numbers in the output file must have an error margin within 5e-3 compared to the correct answer to be considered correct. If any number exceeds this margin, it will be judged as a wrong answer.  
2. **\[20%\] Demo**  
* A demo session will be held remotely. You’ll be asked questions about the homework.  
3. **\[20%\] Report**  
* Grading is based on your evaluation, discussion and writing. If you want to get more points, design or conduct more experiments to analyze your implementation. 

7. # **Submission**

Upload the files below to eeclass. (**DO NOT COMPRESS THEM**)

* hw4.cu  
* hw4.hip (optional)  
* Makefile (optional)  
* hw4\_{student\_ID}.pdf

8. # **Final Notes**

* Type hw4-judge to run the test cases for Nvidia GPU.  
* Type hw4-amd-judge to run the test cases for Amd GPU.  
* If you want to submit your Makefile, **please combine it into a single file**. All of your code should be compiled by make hw4 with the same Makefile.  
* Scoreboard:  
  * [hw4](https://pp.lsalab.cs.nthu.edu.tw/hw4/)  
  * [hw4-amd](https://pp.lsalab.cs.nthu.edu.tw/hw4-amd/)  
* Resources are provided under /home/pp25/share/hw4/ on Apollo-GPU.  
  * seq-attention \- a sequential code of original attention implementation  
  * seq-flashattention \- a sequential code of flashattention implementation  
  * Makefile \- example Makefile  
  * testcases/ \- sample test cases  
* Contact TA via pp@lsalab.cs.nthu.edu.tw or eeclass if you find any problems with the homework specification, judge scripts, example source code or the test cases.  
* You are allowed to discuss and exchange ideas with others, but you are required to write the code on your own. You’ll get **0 points** if we found you cheating.
